reasoning: Method A introduces a Neural CDE to initialize a continuous-time latent for asynchronous sensors, then "samples tokens at observed times" for the transformer backbone. This is a reasonable idea but the integration of Neural CDE outputs into the tokenization pipeline is underspecified—it's unclear how the CDE latent becomes discrete tokens compatible with the shared transformer without additional design choices. More critically, Method A's energy-based cloze loss using conditional noise-contrastive estimation is described as having "a learned, modality-aware noise process proposes replacements for all masked tokens in a single pass," but this requires training a separate noise/proposal model whose design and training procedure are never specified, creating a missing prerequisite. The prefix-style bidirectional/causal hybrid attention is also somewhat underspecified in how it's implemented within a single transformer. Method B is more straightforwardly assembled from well-defined components: Graph Transformers with Laplacian encodings, temporal transformers, mixture density heads, spectral losses, and EMA target encoders are all standard, well-understood components. The optional diffusion-style denoising heads sharing the encoder is a minor paradigm mix but is flagged as optional and doesn't undermine the core design. Method B's components connect more cleanly without requiring undefined sub-systems, making it more implementable as described.
